characters.relation: Carthaginian invader he refused to rush
characters.blurb: Master of Trasimene and Cannae. Fabius’s delays denied him a decisive Roman ruin—and earned Fabius the mockery of hotter Romans.

characters.relation: Master of the Horse who forced a fight
characters.blurb: Made co-dictator by popular anger at delay; his rash advance nearly destroyed the army until Fabius marched to save him.
Pushed the war into Africa against Fabius’s advice. History favored Scipio’s boldness—Plutarch still honors Fabius’s earlier salvation.
Popular general who ignored auspices and Fabius’s temper; Hannibal trapped him at the lake and killed him with the army.
Plebeian consul who forced battle at Cannae against Paulus’s caution; the disaster vindicated Fabius’s strategy too late.
Patrician consul who preferred Fabian delay; died fighting at Cannae after Varro’s aggression.
Aggressive counterpart in the later war—the pair Romans called the shield and sword of the Republic against Hannibal.
Attacked Fabius’s dictatorship in the assembly and carried the law that made Minucius equal in command.
Officer who, looking at the small Roman camp, wondered that they still dared show themselves after so many defeats.
Greek city in Italy Hannibal held by treachery; Fabius retook it and kept his famous refusal to plunder the gods’ colossus lightly.
Region and people caught between Rome and Carthage; Fabius’s operations against Tarentum turn on their betrayals.
Title of Fabius’s house—already great before he became Cunctator by saving Rome through patience.
Called Verrucosus for a wart above the lip, and Ovicula for a soft youthful manner that hid a stubborn will.
Appears among the consular generation that fought on after Cannae while Fabius’s authority still steadied Rome.
Mother city of Hannibal’s war. Fabius feared a young Scipio would leave Italy bare to strike it.

characters.relation: God of a vow after Trasimene
characters.blurb: Fabius vowed a sacred spring and games to Hercules (and other rites) to steady Roman religion after the lake disaster.
Members of the Claudii share the consular years of the Hannibalic war beside Fabius’s dictatorships.

characters.relation: Greek pair in the Parallel Lives
characters.blurb: Pericles is Fabius’s match: each a deliberate leader accused of softness by men who loved quick battles.
The Punic enemy in Italy and Spain; Fabius’s strategy was to wear them down without offering another Cannae.
The name Romans used for the dictator who saved the state by refusing Hannibal’s invitations to ruin.

characters.relation: Young patrician who found Fabius after Cannae
characters.blurb: Recognized Fabius alone and on foot in the ruins after Cannae, dismounted to offer him his horse, and wept at the old man’s calm refusal of the consolation.

characters.relation: Colleague who refused to support Scipio against Fabius
characters.blurb: The Crassus who was Scipio’s consular colleague; Fabius urged him to block Scipio’s Africa expedition, but Crassus declined to move against his partner.

locations.relation: City five times placed in Fabius’s hands as dictator
locations.blurb: Base of five consulships and two dictatorships; Fabius returned here after each campaign to steady morale, conduct vows, and argue against rash decisions.

locations.relation: Region of his first triumph, over the hill people
locations.blurb: In his first consulship Fabius defeated the Ligurians in a pitched battle and drove them to shelter in the Alps—Rome’s first taste of his measured, unhurried force.

locations.relation: Campanian bottleneck where he shadowed and nearly caged Hannibal
locations.blurb: Hannibal’s misdirected march brought him to Casilinum’s enclosed river-plain; Fabius sealed the mountain passes and nearly trapped him until the ox-torches ruse opened a way out.

locations.relation: Greek city he recovered by exploiting a Bruttian love affair
locations.blurb: Using a Tarentine soldier’s sister to suborn the Bruttian garrison commander, Fabius retook the city by night—his finest coup and a pointed rebuke to Hannibal’s own methods.

locations.relation: Village by the Aufidus where Rome’s army was destroyed
locations.blurb: Varro’s impetuous battle-day gave Hannibal his greatest victory; the dead at Cannae vindicated Fabius’s caution to all who had dismissed him as the Cunctator.

locations.relation: Italy’s second city that defected to Hannibal after Cannae
locations.blurb: Capua’s defection—Italy’s richest prize—seemed to confirm that Hannibal should have marched on Rome while he could, exactly the risk Fabius had always warned against.

locations.relation: Town where the surviving consul fled after Cannae
locations.blurb: Consul Varro reached Venusia with a thin remnant; the senate thanked him publicly for not despairing of the republic—a measured grace Plutarch notes as very Roman.

locations.relation: Toe-of-Italy garrison used as Tarentum diversion
locations.blurb: Fabius ordered the Rhegium garrison to raid Bruttian land and storm Caulonia—a feint to draw Hannibal south while he moved on Tarentum from the north.

locations.relation: Bruttian town stormed as cover for the Tarentum plan
locations.blurb: The Rhegium detachment was sent against Caulonia with full force, sacrificing the least dependable troops to give Hannibal a false southern emergency.

locations.relation: Province Scipio cleared before striking Africa
locations.blurb: Scipio’s Spanish victories gave Rome the base and prestige for the African invasion Fabius feared would strip Italy bare while Hannibal still roamed it.
